tweets.text:
Which sets up my post on Cleveland Fed President Hammack (an #FOMC voter in 2026 (and every two years unlike many Fed presidents) who also believes that rates are not restrictive) who in a speech in Cleveland (link below) says while for now "it’s reasonable to keep rates steady given the uncertainties around the economic outlook... if recent trends continue, it may soon be appropriate to act... I’m more concerned about the growing risks of persistently elevated inflation than the risks to full employment and also that monetary policy may not be sufficiently restrictive to bring inflation down to 2 percent."

"If we wait for definitive evidence that high inflation has become embedded in the economy, it may require larger policy adjustments, at greater cost."

"the 2 percent objective matters—not as a theoretical construct, but as a practical foundation for sound economic decision-making by businesses, consumers, and investors."

https://t.co/JaFGYKRzLL

tweets.text:
While not an #FOMC voter until 2028, Kansas City Fed President maintained his status last week as one of the most hawkish Fed members (along with Cleveland Fed Pres Hammack) with a BBG interview along with a speech in Iceland (link below) not only agreeing with the majority of the committee that the "primary concern is inflation, which is too hot and has been above target for too long,” but indicating (in the interview) that he thought rates were likely accommodative (which would argue for rate hikes even if inflation were to fall).

From the speech: 

“We must continue to signal our commitment to price stability and our willingness to take the actions necessary to achieve our mandate.”

"I place little stock in assuming that the most recent runup in prices is transitory within an acceptable time horizon."

https://t.co/zmFbkirW5a
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City President Jeff Schmid said he remained focused on elevated inflation https://t.co/1thhRRLxiD
tweets.full_text:
While not an #FOMC voter until 2028, Kansas City Fed President maintained his status last week as one of the most hawkish Fed members (along with Cleveland Fed Pres Hammack) with a BBG interview along with a speech in Iceland (link below) not only agreeing with the majority of the committee that the "primary concern is inflation, which is too hot and has been above target for too long,” but indicating (in the interview) that he thought rates were likely accommodative (which would argue for rate hikes even if inflation were to fall).

From the speech: 

“We must continue to signal our commitment to price stability and our willingness to take the actions necessary to achieve our mandate.”

"I place little stock in assuming that the most recent runup in prices is transitory within an acceptable time horizon."

https://t.co/zmFbkirW5a

tweets.text:
JOLTS quits (considered a measure of labor market tightness — people don’t quit their job unless they are confident they can find another one) also slipped -183k, the largest decline in a year to 2.977mn, the third decline in four months. 

They remain at the bottom of their range over the past two years, just 4k above the least since August 2020.

https://t.co/U4GT1BokC7

tweets.text:
MarketWatch: Stuart Kaiser, head of U.S. equity trading strategy at Citi, says "the oil tail risk outcome has been cut by 3/4 from about the same starting price," since March 27th.

On March 27, when the U.S. Oil Fund was trading above $124, options implied a 6.2% chance it would rally a further 50% in a month. 

Now, with $USO near $128, odds of a 50% gain are just 1.5%.
tweets.full_text:

tweets.text:
From the Week Ahead (https://t.co/dK8IKSbarp):

Earnings revisions, after starting the earnings season with two negative weeks, have remained very healthy for a fifth week in the week of May 22nd, accelerating back to the joint highest since August 2021, marking the best four weeks since then, and seeing the 20-week moving average continue to lift higher again, as 2026 EPS estimates continue to rise to new highs, as they’ve done each week since the turn of the year, now up a stunning ~31% over that period.
